Best Practices for Building a Modern and Resilient Supply Chain Framework
in the Oil and Gas Industry
Akash Sharma, Enverus, Houston, Texas, USA; Dan McDonald, Enverus, Tulsa, Oklahoma, USA

This paper was prepared for presentation at the 2023 SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition held in San Antonio, Texas, USA, 16 - 18 October 2023.

Abstract
The oil and gas industry operates in a complex and challenging environment with significant supply chain
management risks. This paper aims to provide a comprehensive review of these risks and proposes strategies
to create a best-in-class supply chain practice using Industry 4.0 technologies. With energy prices being
highly volatile and the macroeconomic situation created by COVID-19, having a resilient and robust supply
chain practice is essential for oil and gas operators. The paper emphasizes the need for the energy industry
to shift its view of the supply chain from a tactical, reactive organization to one of strategic value.
To establish a best-in-class supply chain practice, the paper discusses the integration of various Industry
4.0 technologies, such as artificial intelligence, robotics, and automated systems. The authors suggest
leveraging tools such as spend analytics and data modeling to make informed decisions and automate
dispatch processes, leading to positive fiscal and ESG impacts. They also emphasize the critical role of
automation of the payables process and real-time visibility of validated invoice line-item cost detail data
to achieve a should-cost modeling environment. The authors propose a manage-by-exception model that
focuses on operations, value creation, process improvement, and automation of redundant support functions
to mitigate risk and provide a competitive advantage for the entire enterprise. The paper presents novel and
additive ideas for modernizing the oil and gas supply chain.
Background – The Why
In the contemporary oil and gas industry landscape, developing a best-in-class SCM system transcends
strategic implications; it is an operational necessity. The risks of falling behind this transformation curve are
far-reaching, potentially leading to operational bottlenecks, escalated costs, and competitive disadvantage.
On the contrary, firms that adapt and adopt new SCM practices will enjoy enhanced operational efficiencies,
cost advantages, and improved strategic positioning.
Theme I: The Indispensable Shift towards Strategic Supply Chain Management
In an era characterized by unstable market dynamics, such as the fluctuating West Texas Intermediate
(WTI) prices and the disruptions instigated by the COVID-19 pandemic, the necessity for a resilient Supply
Chain Management (SCM) system in the oil and gas industry has been brought to the forefront. As these

variables induce an escalating demand for strategic SCM practices, companies are compelled to pivot from
traditionally tactical approaches to more sustainable and strategic ones.
Other industries with tighter operating margins were swift in identifying the strategic value of SCM as
a tool for resource optimization and cost management. Conversely, the energy industry has been slower in
acknowledging this value. The urgency for a robust, strategic supply chain has become apparent in light of
recent market volatility and the global impacts of COVID-19. Companies that neglect the development of
sustainable SCM practices expose themselves to the risks of market volatility and global crises, potentially
endangering both their operational and financial stability.
Theme II: Harnessing Industry 4.0 for a Sustainable Supply Chain
Industry 4.0 or the Fourth Industrial Revolution ushers in a slew of technological advancements, such as
automation, data exchange, and the Internet of Things (IoT), enabling companies to explore sustainable and
efficient SCM practices. Embracing these technologies can bolster supply chain efficiency, resilience, and
strategic value, leading to sustainable digital transformation.
The advantages of a successful digital transformation are substantial, offering enhanced operational
control, enriched insights, and increased transparency across commercial support functions. However, the
path to digital transformation is rife with challenges, from managing change and enhancing communication
to understanding existing systems. Companies that hesitate to undertake this transformation risk operational
stagnation, diminished transparency, and compromised competitiveness.
Theme III: Capitalizing on Digitization and the Dire Consequences of Inefficiency
The energy industry stands at the brink of a disruption, with the potential for automation and digitization of
supply chain operations creating opportunities for improved sustainability and profitability. Early adopters
are more likely to navigate their digital transformations successfully, whereas those reluctant to change risk
lagging behind in operational efficiency and profit optimization.
Moreover, failure to integrate and automate all business support functions on a unified platform can
prevent the creation of a value-centric ecosystem, restricting real-time visibility crucial for adaptive planning
in a volatile industry. Efficient and automated systems, conversely, can enhance industry responsiveness
and foster sustainable profitability.
Theme IV: Integrating ESG Goals into Supply Chain Management
As companies strive to meet the escalating demands for sustainability, the integration of Environmental,
Social, and Governance (ESG) considerations into SCM has become a critical element. In addition to carbon
offset purchases, corporations need to make substantive strides towards reducing their carbon footprints and
increasing transparency with investors. Companies that can meet these ESG goals and emission reduction
targets have the potential to trade at higher valuations, ultimately contributing to improved financial
performance and investment attractiveness.
Methodology – The How
Establishing a "Best-In-Class" Supply Chain organization is often a complex endeavor, with no universal
formula for success. However, we aim to distill several key concepts in this section that may guide an oil and
gas company from its existing ecosystem and strategy towards a considerably more resilient and advanced
one. No matter where a company currently positions itself on the spectrum from nascent to advanced supply
chain operations, the adoption of some, or ideally all, of these concepts promises to usher in substantial
enhancements in their supply chain strategy. As previously discussed, these improvements have the potential
to unlock significant value, thereby bolstering the overall operational efficiency of the organization.

Concept I: Digitalization and Automation in Supply Chain Management (SCM)
The impact of digital transformation in Supply Chain Management (SCM) necessitates a deep understanding
of two integral aspects: digitalization and automation. These transformative elements can significantly boost
the supply chain's efficiency and equip organizations with the agility required to adapt to variable market
conditions and consumer demands. By facilitating seamless transactions and information flow, they pave
the way for a robust, resilient, and dynamic supply chain.
Digitalization in the context of SCM refers to the strategic employment of digital technologies to convert
information into a digital format. This process is primarily concerned with harnessing the power of data,
transforming it into actionable insights, and enabling the automation of supply chain processes. At the heart
of digitalization is the challenge of ensuring data integrity and accessibility. High-quality data is the lifeblood
of digital supply chains, providing the foundation for functions such as spend analytics and data modeling.
These tools can yield invaluable insights into the operational efficiency of the supply chain, offering a clear
picture of where improvements can be made. Digitalization underpins strategic decision-making and risk
mitigation, making it a cornerstone of effective SCM.
The concept of automation is deeply entwined with digitalization. It is concerned with the use of
technologies like Artificial Intelligence (AI), Machine Learning (ML), and robotics to expedite and
streamline supply chain operations. From warehouse management to delivery, automation can bring
about revolutionary changes, enhancing efficiency, reducing errors, and thereby bolstering both fiscal
and Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) outcomes. By transforming specific processes such
as dispatch through ticketing, accounts payable, and invoicing, automation can generate considerable
efficiency gains, not to mention cost savings. By automating routine tasks, staff can focus more on strategic
areas, thereby further driving organizational growth. In the energy industry, these technological shifts are
not just advantageous—they're imperative for long-term sustainability and competitiveness.
Looking specifically at the payables process part of supply chain management, automation presents itself
as a compelling strategic move. Particularly for those transactions that involve low-value invoices, the
benefits of automation are immense. Automating this aspect of the process frees up valuable time for various
internal teams, including engineering, accounting, and senior management, and allows them to focus on
tasks that carry a higher value and strategic relevance. In traditional models, these teams often get mired in
routine transactional tasks, such as price validation, scope and quantity verification, and coding validation.
Automating these tasks could streamline operations significantly, ensuring faster processing times, fewer
errors, and overall efficiency enhancement.
While the upfront investment in technology might seem substantial, the long-term benefits offered by
automation certainly outweigh the initial costs. When payables processes are automated, companies can
redirect their human capital towards high-value tasks such as cost analysis, financial forecasting, and budget
control. Automation could also help in eliminating manual errors, improving compliance, and increasing
transparency, making the entire payables process more effective and manageable. Thus, an investment
in automating the payables process is essentially an investment in future operational efficiency, cost-
effectiveness, and value creation.
Concept II: Emphasis on a ‘Manage-by-Exception’ Model
The ‘Manage-by-Exception’ model stands as a cornerstone in modern supply chain management, creating
a shift in business operations towards more strategic and value-added activities. By automating routine
tasks and inviting human intervention only when anomalies arise or predefined parameters are breached,
this model effectively optimizes the utilization of resources. Exceptions can include a myriad of issues,
such as supply chain disruptions, inventory discrepancies, delivery delays, or quality concerns, to which the
organization must swiftly respond to maintain operational flow.
The benefits of the ‘manage-by-exception’ model are numerous, offering a rapid response mechanism
to potential problems, thus reducing their overall impact on operations and leading to cost savings and

Data forms the core of cost modeling, and therefore, using reliable, timely data is key to developing
effective models. In the age of digitalization, organizations have access to an ever-increasing volume of
data, but it's crucial that this data be of high quality and easily accessible. A flawed or unreliable data set
can lead to inaccurate cost models, which in turn can cause misguided strategies and financial losses. On
the other hand, if used correctly, accurate data can reveal inefficiencies, bottlenecks, and opportunities for
improvement. Consequently, the emphasis should be on building robust data infrastructure and cultivating
a data-driven culture. A strong, data-driven cost model can not only result in cost optimization but also lead
to improved supplier negotiations, enhanced quality control, and efficient resource allocation. Ultimately,
it helps organizations stay competitive in a rapidly evolving market.
Concept IV: Data Integrity is King – For Analytics and Governance
The pivotal role of data integrity and accessibility in digitalized supply chain management cannot be
overstressed. Essentially, it's the foundation of digital transformation in SCM, underpinning every decision,
strategy, and subsequent action. For an organization to transition successfully into the digital era, it must
ensure that it has a robust mechanism in place to gather accurate, reliable, and actionable data from a plethora
of sources.
Data integrity refers to the accuracy and consistency of data over its entire lifecycle. High data integrity
guarantees that the information is exactly as intended, free from any corruption or alteration from the
original. It is the prerequisite for data reliability and the cornerstone for data-driven decision-making. This is
especially crucial in SCM, as decisions made based on flawed or inconsistent data can lead to inefficiencies,
decreased productivity, and potentially, financial losses.
On the other hand, data accessibility refers to how readily available and understandable data is to
those who need it. With easily accessible data, organizations can make swift, informed decisions, leading
to enhanced agility and resilience. Accessible data also facilitates transparency, fostering trust among
stakeholders. However, managing these data aspects can be a daunting task due to the sheer volume
and complexity of the information being processed. Therefore, it's essential for organizations to adopt
sophisticated data management tools, and possibly, AI and machine learning technologies, to ensure data
integrity and accessibility while gleaning valuable insights for strategic operations. After all, the power of
digitalization in SCM lies in harnessing data to its full potential – transforming raw data into actionable
intelligence for optimal operational outcomes.
Concept V: Strategic Sourcing and Category Management
Strategic sourcing and category management form an integral part of efficient supply chain management,
particularly in the context of the oil and gas industry. These techniques, when bolstered by data-driven
insights, can foster proactive decision-making, cost optimization, and risk mitigation - the holy grail of any
robust SCM framework.
Strategic sourcing is a systematic process, allowing companies to reevaluate and optimize their
purchasing activities. The focus is on improving overall business performance by understanding total
spending, reducing costs, streamlining operations, and improving the quality of products and services. It's
not just about obtaining the lowest price but about establishing long-term partnerships with suppliers to
generate sustained value. This process, when armed with relevant, accurate data, can unearth significant
opportunities for operational improvements and cost savings.
Meanwhile, category management goes a step further, helping organizations manage their supply
categories as strategic business units. This practice revolves around sourcing strategically important
categories that drive significant value to the business. Category management involves an end-to-end
approach, right from identifying the need for a particular category to finalizing contracts with suppliers.
Using a data-driven approach, category management aims at maximizing value from the supply base,

Your data set could also power your strategic sourcing and category management tools. Effective category
management, similar to a strategic approach in managing a group of products or services, relies on analyzing
market trends, understanding customer needs, and developing strategies to optimize procurement and
pricing. Your commercial documentation can flip into a pricing validation tool, much like the body's immune
system, ensuring you are being charged as per your negotiations and affirming control over your spend.
Picture all these tools feeding into a management-by-exception dashboard with multi-level visibility and
drill-down capabilities. This ‘vision’ could facilitate supplier network visibility, supplier onboarding, risk
vetting, and CapEx/OpEx budget tracking. Eventually, this could enable should-cost modeling and scenario
planning, while easy access to market intelligence would provide insights on how external events could
impact your supply chain.
In an increasingly agile world, you need a resilient supply chain that can withstand the unexpected. A
future-facing organization would leverage this ecosystem to replace tactical paper-pushing with strategic,
data-informed decision-making. The effectiveness of this digital ecosystem depends on the quality of
the data input; inferior data will invariably lead to inferior insights, which in turn will impact decision-
making. Just like a healthy body relies on a balanced diet, a robust digital brain ecosystem is fundamentally
contingent on the quality of the data it processes.
Conclusions
In conclusion, this paper has explored the critical need for the oil and gas industry to develop a best-in-
class supply chain management (SCM) practice in the face of complex and volatile market dynamics. By
embracing Industry 4.0 technologies and shifting from a tactical to a strategic approach, companies can
achieve enhanced operational efficiencies, cost optimization, and improved strategic positioning.
The integration of various Industry 4.0 technologies, such as artificial intelligence, robotics, and
automated systems, is essential for achieving a resilient and advanced supply chain practice. Tools like
spend analytics and data modeling enable informed decision-making and automation of processes, leading
to positive fiscal and Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) impacts. Automation of the payables
process and real-time visibility of validated invoice line-item cost detail data can drive efficiency and
establish a should-cost modeling environment.
The paper also highlights the importance of adopting a "manage-by-exception" model, focusing on
operations, value creation, process improvement, and automation of redundant support functions. This
approach optimizes resource utilization and enables rapid response to supply chain disruptions, leading to
cost savings and improved service levels.
Additionally, the strategic use of cost modeling and data-driven decision making allows organizations
to identify areas of potential savings, enhance operational efficiency, and mitigate risks. Data integrity and
accessibility are critical for successful digital transformation, empowering organizations to harness the full
potential of data for actionable insights and optimal decision-making.
Furthermore, integrating Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) considerations into supply chain
management is crucial in today's sustainability-focused landscape. Meeting ESG goals and reducing carbon
footprints contribute to improved financial performance and investment attractiveness.
The concept of a source-to-pay (S2P) strategy emerges as a key enabler for a best-in-class supply chain
practice. Integrating strategic sourcing, category management, and automation in the S2P process enhances
operational efficiency, cost optimization, and risk management. The S2P process serves as a critical driver
of business value, facilitating efficient supplier management, real-time visibility, and improved decision-
making.
Finally, the paper presents the Digital Brain ecosystem, an analogy comparing the interconnected
elements of supply chain management to the various components of the human body. Data acts as the fuel
for the digital brain, driving informed decision-making, while automation and analytics tools play vital roles

in streamlining processes and providing insights. The effectiveness of this ecosystem relies on the quality
of the data input, akin to a healthy body depending on a balanced diet.
By adopting the concepts and strategies discussed in this paper, the oil and gas industry can modernize its
supply chain, achieve operational excellence, and gain a competitive advantage in an increasingly dynamic
and demanding market. The journey toward a best-in-class supply chain practice is a transformative one,
requiring organizational commitment, investment in technology, and a data-driven culture. However, the
potential rewards in terms of efficiency, cost savings, risk mitigation, and sustainability make it a worthwhile
endeavor for companies seeking long-term success.
